topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notification blinked on my phone, a stark intrusion into the quiet afternoon. A package, addressed to the former resident of Apartment 3B, was waiting by the door. My stomach tightened, a cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I hesitated, my hand hovering over the cardboard box. It wasn't just the unexpected nature of it; it was the sheer wrongness of it. I glanced around the hallway, the familiar paint and muted lighting suddenly feeling thin, like a poorly stretched sheet. When I finally picked it up, the tape felt strangely sticky under my fingers. A faint, almost imperceptible scent—something metallic and faintly floral—wafted up, making me instinctively take a half-step back. I didn't want to open it.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked behind the mail slot, a brown rectangle sitting incongruously against the grey concrete. My breath hitched as I approached it, my pace slowing until I was almost stationary. Every nerve ending seemed to be vibrating with a low thrum of apprehension. Who would send something to someone who wasn't there anymore? I circled the door twice, my gaze darting to the peephole, though I knew the camera wouldn't show much. A sudden tremor ran through my hands; I gripped the box tightly, the paper crinkling loudly in the silence. I considered leaving it there, letting the mystery fester, but the urge to know what was inside was surprisingly strong, a demanding pull I couldn't seem to resist.

[story 3]

The doorbell rang, a jarring sound that snapped me out of my contemplation. It was a delivery, and there it was: a medium-sized box clearly marked with an old apartment number. As I took the package from the courier, my posture stiffened. I kept my eyes fixed on the street outside, scanning the shadows pooled beneath the fire escape. A persistent, hollow feeling settled in my chest, heavy and suffocating. I carried the box into my living room, setting it down on the coffee table with exaggerated care. I didn't touch it immediately. Instead, I paced the length of the room, running a hand through my hair repeatedly, searching for any logical explanation for this unsolicited arrival. The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic drumming in my ears.
